title: Playwright Parallel Execution: When to Shard and When to Scale Workers
excerpt: Sharding and workers both promise faster test runs in Playwright, but they solve different problems — and mixing them up will cost you time and CI minutes.
content:
Most teams I've worked with reach for parallelism in Playwright the moment their test suite crosses the five-minute mark. That instinct is right, but the execution often isn't. They either throw more workers at the problem or blindly enable sharding based on a blog post, without understanding what each mechanism actually does. A few months ago I helped a team cut their GitHub Actions pipeline from 18 minutes down to 6 — not by adding more resources, but by switching from the wrong parallelism strategy to the right one. The distinction between workers and sharding is the kind of thing that looks trivial in the docs but has a real impact on wall-clock time, flakiness, and CI cost.

Let me walk through both mechanisms, how they interact, and when I'd choose one over the other.

## Workers: Parallelism Within a Single Machine

![](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1461749280684-dccba630e2f6?w=800&q=80)


When you set `workers` in your `playwright.config.ts`, you're telling Playwright how many browser processes to spin up simultaneously on the machine running your tests. By default, Playwright 1.43 uses half the available CPU cores, which is a reasonable starting point but often not optimal.

```typescript
// playwright.config.ts
export default defineConfig({
  workers: process.env.CI ? 4 : 2,
  use: {
    baseURL: 'http://localhost:3000',
  },
});
```

The important thing to understand about workers is that they share the same machine's resources — CPU, memory, and network bandwidth. This matters more than most people expect. I've seen setups where bumping workers from 4 to 8 actually *increased* total run time because the GitHub Actions runner (a 2-core machine by default) was thrashing under the load. The tests were technically running in parallel, but context switching and memory pressure made each individual test slower.

A good rule of thumb I've settled on: set workers to the number of physical cores available, not logical cores. On a 4-core GitHub Actions runner (the `ubuntu-latest` large runner), `workers: 4` is usually the ceiling before you start seeing diminishing returns. You can validate this empirically by running your suite with different worker counts and comparing total wall-clock time — something most teams never bother doing.

Workers also have a significant advantage: they share the same process context. If you're using global setup to seed a database or spin up a local server, that setup runs once regardless of worker count. This is not the case with sharding, which I'll get to in a moment.

One more thing worth knowing about workers: Playwright distributes tests across workers using a first-come-first-served model based on test file ordering. If you have a few extremely long-running test files, they can create a straggler problem — most workers finish early while one is still grinding through a 300-line spec. You can partially address this by splitting large spec files or using `--shard` to distribute more evenly, but it's an architectural decision worth making consciously.

## Sharding: Parallelism Across Multiple Machines

Sharding is a fundamentally different concept. Instead of running more tests simultaneously on one machine, you split your test suite across multiple machines (or CI jobs), each running a subset of tests. Every shard is an independent Playwright process with its own workers.

The canonical GitHub Actions setup looks like this:

```yaml
jobs:
  test:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        shardIndex: [1, 2, 3, 4]
        shardTotal: [4]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npx playwright install --with-deps chromium
      - run: npx playwright test --shard=${{ matrix.shardIndex }}/${{ matrix.shardTotal }}
```

Each of those four jobs runs independently and in parallel. If your full suite takes 20 minutes on a single machine, four shards can theoretically cut that to 5 minutes — assuming even distribution, which Playwright handles by splitting test files as evenly as possible by count.

The "by count" part is the key limitation. Playwright 1.43 distributes test *files* across shards, not individual test cases. If shard 1 gets your `auth.spec.ts` with 3 tests and shard 2 gets your `checkout.spec.ts` with 47 tests, the distribution is wildly uneven and you won't get the speedup you're expecting. I've debugged this exact situation on a project where we had a monolithic `e2e.spec.ts` file that one shard always got stuck with. The fix was straightforward: break large spec files into smaller, more focused ones. Not just for sharding, but for maintainability too.

Sharding also changes how you handle global setup. Each shard runs its own `globalSetup`, which means if your global setup hits a shared database, you can run into race conditions or redundant seeding. We handle this by making our global setup idempotent — it checks whether the seed data exists before writing it — and using a test user per shard pattern to avoid cross-contamination between parallel runs.

## Combining Sharding and Workers: the Configuration That Actually Makes Sense

![](https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1537432376769-00f5c2f4c8d2?w=800&q=80)


You can and should use both. Sharding determines how many CI jobs you spin up. Workers determines how many browsers run in parallel within each shard. The right combination depends on your runner specs and suite characteristics.

For a suite of 400 tests running on standard GitHub Actions `ubuntu-latest` runners:

```typescript
export default defineConfig({
  workers: process.env.CI ? 2 : 1,
  // Additional config...
});
```

```yaml
# 8 shards × 2 workers = 16 concurrent browsers
matrix:
  shardIndex: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8]
  shardTotal: [8]
```

This gives you 16 browser processes total, distributed across 8 machines with 2 workers each. Compare this to running 16 workers on a single machine — the distributed approach is consistently faster because you're not fighting for CPU and memory on one box.

That said, more shards isn't always better. Each shard incurs startup overhead: checking out the repo, installing dependencies, running Playwright's browser install. On our project, that overhead is about 90 seconds per shard. At 8 shards, that's a fixed cost you're paying regardless of test count. Below a certain suite size, fewer shards with more workers per shard is actually faster. I've found the crossover point is usually somewhere around 150–200 tests for a typical web app — below that, 2–4 workers on a single machine outperforms 4 shards.

## Reporting and Blob Merging: the Part Everyone Forgets

Once you split your test suite across shards, you lose unified reporting unless you explicitly merge results. Playwright 1.37 introduced blob reporters for exactly this purpose, and it's one of the better-designed features in recent releases.

Each shard produces a blob artifact:

```yaml
- run: npx playwright test --shard=${{ matrix.shardIndex }}/${{ matrix.shardTotal }} --reporter=blob
- uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
  with:
    name: blob-report-${{ matrix.shardIndex }}
    path: blob-report
```

A separate merge job then downloads all blobs and produces a single HTML report:

```yaml
merge-reports:
  needs: test
  runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  steps:
    - uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
      with:
        path: all-blob-reports
        pattern: blob-report-*
        merge-multiple: true
    - run: npx playwright merge-reports --reporter html ./all-blob-reports
    - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
      with:
        name: html-report
        path: playwright-report
```

Skipping this step is the most common mistake I see with sharded setups. You end up with 8 separate HTML reports, no unified view of failures, and a painful debugging experience. The blob merge adds maybe 30 seconds to your pipeline and makes post-failure investigation dramatically easier.

## Making the Decision

If you're working on a test suite under 150 tests running on a reasonably spec'd CI machine, start with workers and skip sharding entirely. Tune the worker count empirically — run your suite at 2, 4, and 8 workers and measure. The results will probably surprise you.

If you're pushing past 200 tests and your build time is the main constraint, add sharding. Start with 4 shards before reaching for 8 or more — the law of diminishing returns hits faster than most people expect once startup overhead becomes a significant portion of total runtime.

And if you have a mix of fast unit-style tests and slow end-to-end flows, consider splitting them into separate Playwright projects with different shard configurations. That's a more advanced setup, but it gives you granular control over where CI resources actually go.

The real lesson from all the pipeline debugging I've done: measure before you configure, not after. Playwright's `--reporter=json` output gives you per-test timing data that makes it straightforward to identify bottlenecks. Use it before you start tweaking worker counts or adding shards — you'll almost always find that the problem isn't where you expected it to be.
